retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with diabetes who receive teprotumumab are advised to have monitoring of blood glucose and glycated hemoglobin levels and should be co-managed with an endocrinologist. Tests recommended include fasting blood glucose after each of the first two infusions and self-monitoring at least twice a day.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Teprotumumab has no listed contraindications on the label. However, warnings and precautions are for poorly controlled diabetics, pregnant or breastfeeding women, and prepubertal children. Based on its mechanism of action, it is likely that teprotumumab will have teratogenic effects during pregnancy. Therefore, it is recommended that women of child-bearing age use adequate contraception before starting therapy, during the course of treatment, and for six months following the last dose of teprotumumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Adverse Effects -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperglycemia is identified as related to drug action for teprotumumab. In patients who did not have underlying diabetes, hyperglycemia was mild (55 to 70 mg/dl), intermittent, and occurred at similar rates in the placebo and teprotumumab groups. Moderate or severe hyperglycemia occurred in some patients with diabetes who received teprotumumab, which was well controlled after adjusting the medication for diabetes. Hemoglobin A1c levels returned to baseline after cessation of therapy. The OPTIC trial reported two grade 1 hyperglycemia cases (<5%) which resolved spontaneously. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: An endocrinologist to render the patient euthyroid and manage patients who may develop secondary hyperglycemia An ophthalmologist to adequately examine the eye, ascertain which patients may be good candidates for teprotumumab, and monitor the progression A rheumatologist for medical workups, drug prescription, and managing drug infusion reactions in a day center A pharmacist to assist with dosing A radiologist to assess treatment response (e.g., possible reduction of muscle or fat volume, improvement of apex crowding) A gastroenterologist to manage patients who may develop IBD Psychosocial support Teprotumumab has shown great promise to change the landscape of managing thyroid eye disease, and working in an interprofessional team will better patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Teprotumumab is a novel targeted treatment for thyroid eye disease that has shown encouraging results in decreasing the inflammatory process, reversing disease endpoints such as diplopia and proptosis, and reducing the need for surgical intervention. [57] The drug may be considered first-line therapy for patients with clinically significant ophthalmopathy. The CAS may be valid for longitudinal monitoring but should not be used to establish treatment eligibility. [56] The use of teprotumumab requires an interprofessional team of healthcare professionals, which may include:

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Teprotumumab for thyroid eye disease should be used cautiously in patients with IBD. In the phase 2 study for thyroid orbitopathy, two patients with pre-existing colitis showed severe exacerbations while receiving teprotumumab. These adverse events led the researchers to exclude patients with IBD from the phase 3 study and for the FDA to list IBD as a relative contraindication. It can be considered in selected patients; however, co-management with a gastroenterologist is strongly recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Indications -- Dysthyroid Optic Neuropathy (DON)
retrieved_docs.content: Ten patients with DON were eligible for inclusion in this study. Seventy percent of patients treated with teprotumumab had an objective improvement in DON after two infusions, measured as significant improvement in visual acuity, resolution of RAPD, or both. [17] Seven patients who presented with color vision deficits had subsequent normalization or improvement of color vision, and three patients with long-standing DON and hand movement vision had minimal objective improvement.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Indications -- Pooled and Extended Outcome Analysis in Active Moderate to Severe Thyroid Eye Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The extended outcome of the two teprotumumab randomized controlled trials was reported, with up to 18 months of follow-up (72 weeks). By pooling the intention-to-treat population from the phase 2 and phase 3 teprotumumab randomized controlled trials, significant efficacy for teprotumumab was revealed where numbers-needed-to-treat were 1.6 for proptosis response, 2.5 for diplopia response, 1.7 for the overall response, and 2.5 for disease inactivation. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for teprotumumab. Describe its mechanism of action. Understand the most common adverse events associated with teprotumumab therapy. Explain the importance of monitoring for patients on teprotumumab. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Adverse Effects -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: OPTIC-X study reported three other cases of hyperglycemia (8.1%) in the OPTIC placebo group who later received teprotumumab, two with new-onset type 2 diabetes, and one with a transient increase in blood glucose. [6] Teprotumumab shows no detectable affinity for the insulin receptor; thus, the secondary hyperglycemia is likely linked to IGF-IR inhibition. [35] [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Teprotumumab is a newly approved medication to manage active, moderate to severe thyroid eye disease. Teprotumumab has been approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for treating thyroid orbitopathy, under breakthrough therapy designation, as an Orphan Drug. It is an insulin-like growth factor-1 receptor (IGF-1R) inhibitor in the class of biological therapy. This activity describes the indications and evidence supporting each indication, action, and contraindications for teprotumumab as a valuable agent in treating thyroid eye disease. Furthermore, this activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors (e.g., off-label uses, dosing, pharmacodynamics, monitoring, relevant interactions) pertinent for interprofessional team members in the care of patients with thyroid orbitopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Simultaneous use of teprotumumab with another biological agent is strongly discouraged. No data currently exists regarding potential infectious risks with concomitant treatment. Rituximab-treated patients may have depleted B-cell counts for six months following their last treatment; hence a 6-month break between the last rituximab dose and the start of teprotumumab is strongly advised. The peripheral B-cell count can be checked beforehand.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Indications -- Pooled and Extended Outcome Analysis in Active Moderate to Severe Thyroid Eye Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Proptosis reduction of at least 2 mm was achieved in 77% (65 of 84 patients) treated with teprotumumab, compared to 15% (13 of 87) in placebo at 24 weeks. By 51 weeks post final drug infusion, the maintenance of proptosis response was 67% (38 of 57 patients) in patients with long-term data. Diplopia score improvement by one grade or more was reported in 70% (46 of 66 patients) of the teprotumumab group, compared to 31% (18 of 59 patients) in the placebo group at week 24, with reports of complete resolution of diplopia in 53% in the teprotumumab group compared with 25% in the placebo group. By 51 weeks, 69% (33 of 48 patients) had maintained their diplopia response. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The efficacy and therapeutic index of teprotumumab are assessable with subjective and objective findings. Before starting the drug, the following should be undertaken: A thorough history (in particular, a history of diabetes and IBD). Physical examination (e.g., measurement of weight and blood pressure). Complete ophthalmologic examination (including visual acuity, color vision, exophthalmometry, extraocular motility, anterior segment, and fundus examination). Standard clinical laboratory evaluation (e.g., complete blood count, liver function tests, fasting blood glucose, and hemoglobin A1c). Objective audiology testing (if available). [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Indications -- Chronic, Low CAS Thyroid Eye Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The follow-up duration post-completion of treatment was 16 weeks for 5 of the nine patients. The mean proptosis reduction was 4mm in all patients immediately post-treatment and 4.2 mm in the worse eye at 16 weeks, while the better eye proptosis reduction was 3 mm. [9] Nevertheless, 4 of the nine patients had rehabilitative surgeries after teprotumumab therapy. Ugradar et al. demonstrated a reduction in proptosis in 4 patients with non-inflammatory thyroid eye disease (CAS ≤ 1, for at least four months) following eight infusions of teprotumumab over 24 weeks. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Contraindications -- Warnings and Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications also exist, such as gallbladder disease or diabetic retinopathy. [6] Tirzepatide is only approved for those with T2DM and should not be used for those with T1DM. Furthermore, tirzepatide does not have approval for other forms of diabetes, like latent autoimmune diabetes in adults. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: sulfonylureas (glibenclamide, glimepiride, tolbutamide; torasemide is not a sulfonylurea), biguanides (fenformin/metformin), DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin/sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (exenatid, liraglutide, lixisenatide). Fixed‑dose combinations that pair basal insulins with GLP‑1 analogues—insulin degludec/liraglutide and insulin glargine/lixisenatide—illustrate contemporary strategies to enhance glycaemic control. Growth‑hormone‑based therapies are represented by somatropin and its agonists, including mecasermin, sermorelin, somapacitan and tesamorelin, which function as growth‑hormone analogues or secretagogues. The thyrotropin class contains thyreotropin alfa, a synthetic thyroid‑stimulating hormone analogue used diagnostically and therapeutically. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications link specific compounds to their mechanisms, chemical families, and clinical applications, providing a structured framework

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: of these compounds enables clinicians to perform comprehensive risk assessments, optimize dosing, implement safety measures, and conduct vigilant monitoring to minimize harm while achieving therapeutic objectives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2075
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid and glimepiride are both members of the sulfonylurea class of oral agents used for blood glucose lowering. As sulfonylureas, they act by stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion, thereby helping to control hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing that glibenklamid and glimepiride belong to the same therapeutic group informs clinicians about their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for interchangeable use in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2275
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon, a key metabolic hormone, can be managed with the therapeutic approach identified as “رد فعل تأقي,” while the medication referred to as “ورم جزيري” is contraindicated for use in patients receiving glucagon, indicating that this drug should be avoided to prevent adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 711
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of antidiabetic agents that includes both exenatid and liraglutid. In addition to being a member of the GLP‑1 analogue class, liraglutid is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide, which pairs basal insulin degludec with the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutid to provide complementary mechanisms for glucose lowering. This relationship highlights how individual GLP‑1 analogues can serve both as standalone therapies and as components of combination regimens designed to enhance glycemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: the therapeutic landscape for diabetes includes insulin‑based regimens (particularly long‑acting injectable insulins and fixed‑dose combinations that pair insulin with GLP‑1 analogues) and a variety of oral and injectable non‑insulin agents—biguanides, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, and GLP‑1 analogues—each targeting different pathophysiological pathways to achieve optimal glycaemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 267
retrieved_docs.content: insulin degludec/liraglutide contains the GLP‑1 analogue liraglutide, and insulin glargine/lixisenatide contains the GLP‑1 analogue lixisenatide. This integration of an insulin with a GLP‑1 analogue provides both basal insulin coverage and incretin‑mediated glucose control in a single injection. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how diabetes pharmacotherapy is organized: insulin‑based therapies are distinguished from non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, and within each group, drugs are further classified by mechanism, duration of action, and formulation, facilitating precise selection and combination of therapies for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1647
retrieved_docs.content: التصلب العصيدي is linked to ذَبْهَةُ برنزميتال, indicating a possible association between this vascular hardening disorder and that specific condition. Clinically, التصلب العصيدي is associated with several observable findings: the presence of ال​نفخة الانقباضية (a systolic murmur), لغط الشريان السباتي (a carotid bruit), and the need for حفظ الصحة الروتيني (routine health maintenance) to monitor and manage disease progression. In addition, التصلب العصيدي has a recognized subtype, التصلب العصيدي المعمم, which represents a more generalized form of the disease. Pharmacologically, the PCSK9 inhibitor alirocumab may prevent the development or progression of التصلب العصيدي, suggesting a therapeutic role for lipid‑lowering strategies in patients at risk for this condition. Together, these relationships highlight the clinical manifestations, related conditions, disease subtypes, and potential preventive treatment options relevant to the management of التصلب العصيدي.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise the agents exenatide, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Liraglutide is a component of the fixed‑dose combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, while lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. The category “Insulins and analogues for injection, long‑acting” includes insulin glargine, the combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide. These relationships illustrate how GLP‑1 receptor agonists are used both as individual therapies and as integral parts of long‑acting basal insulin combinations, providing complementary mechanisms to improve glycaemic control in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Insulins and analogues for injection, long-acting is a therapeutic category that includes insulin glargine as a standalone long‑acting insulin analogue and the fixed‑dose combination insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs the basal insulin with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist lixisenatide. Both agents are classified as long‑acting injectable insulins and are used to provide basal glucose control in patients with diabetes, with the combination also offering post‑prandial glucose reduction through lixisenatide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes). Patients with type 1 diabetes have a heightened risk of severe peripheral vascular disease and foot complications, which can progress to the need for an above‑knee amputation. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians to implement early screening, aggressive glycemic control, and multidisciplinary foot‑care strategies aimed at preventing major limb loss and improving overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s medical knowledge classifies drugs used in diabetes into two principal subclasses: insulins and analogues, and non‑insulin blood‑glucose‑lowering agents. Insulins and analogues include long‑acting injectable formulations such as insulin glargine, the fixed‑ratio combinations insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide; liraglutide and lixisenatide also belong to the glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue class, which enhances glucose‑dependent insulin secretion. Non‑insulin agents are organized by mechanism: alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors delay carbohydrate absorption; dipeptidyl‑peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors—alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the combination simvastatin / sitagliptin—prolong incretin activity; sulfonylureas for blood‑glucose lowering—glibenclamide, glimepiride, and tolbutamide—stimulate pancreatic insulin release; biguanides, represented by metformin, reduce hepatic glucose production and improve peripheral insulin sensitivity. Tolbutamide is additionally listed among diagnostic agents for diabetes testing. This taxonomy links insulin‑based therapy with a diverse array of mechanistic drug classes and specific combinations, supporting individualized patient management in diabetes mellitus.

Alirocumab, a lipid‑lowering therapy, may prevent the development or progression of التصلب العصيدي and is implicated in therapeutic interactions with the mechanisms encompassed by احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ. Additionally, التهاب عضل القلب/التهاب التامور غير المعدي (non‑infectious myocarditis and pericarditis) is included in the مرض تأموري group, further emphasizing the integration of inflammatory cardiac disorders within the same cardiovascular disease framework.
